tier_1_full: Layer 4.525 (Entity-Query Overlap Boost) — stop word filtering strips query semantics, highest coupling to all query types
Layer 0.6 (Temporal Fact Lookup) — expanded temporal gate with 4 fallback strategies, high blast radius on temporal queries
Layer 4 (RRF Fusion) — central score aggregation, all boosts compound through this
tier_2_standard: Layer 3 (Vector Search + Query Decomposition) — decomposition correct for multi-hop but adds complexity
Layer 4.55 (Temporal Fact Boost) — TEMPORAL_RECALL_BOOST=0.20 interacts with temporal gate expansion
Layer 4.52 (Experience-Type Boost) — decision query detection could false-positive on single_hop queries
tier_3_light: Layer 4.6 (Interference-Aware Scoring) — neutral on fresh benchmarks
Layer 4.9 (Ontological Re-Ranking) — conservative, unlikely regression source

edge_analysis.data_crossing: Query text → stemmed terms → HashSet<String>; Memory entities → stemmed → Vec<String>; Overlap count → additive boost (0.10*n, cap 0.40)
edge_analysis.control_flow: Always fires when query_stems is non-empty (guard lowered from >=2 to >=1). Iterates all fused candidates.
failure_modes: CRITICAL: Stop word list includes 'use', 'did', 'what', 'when', 'which', 'from', 'have', 'about', 'after', 'before'. These are content-bearing in retrieval queries. 'What programming language did we choose?' → stems to {'program', 'languag', 'choos'} after stripping 'what', 'did'. The word 'use' is stripped, so 'What database are we using?' loses the verb.

CRITICAL: Guard lowered to >=1 overlap means ANY memory with a single incidental stem match gets +0.10 boost. With 60 memories, many will have at least one common stem (e.g., 'test', 'decis', 'chang').

Stemming collapses semantically distinct terms: 'testing' and 'test' both become 'test', boosting test-related memories for 'What testing framework?' but also for 'load testing results' and 'test coverage'.
Entity stems use full entity string stemmed as one token ('rust' → 'rust'), but multi-word entities like 'decision-making' → 'decision-mak' which won't match query stem 'decis'.
edge_analysis.contracts: Boost is additive, capped at 0.40. No normalization against fused score magnitude. A 0.40 boost on a base score of 0.05 creates 9x amplification.
edge_analysis.attack_surface: Entity-stuffed memories could game boosting by including many common terms as entities/tags.

fractal_analysis.pattern_at_scale: Other boost layers (4.5, 4.55, 4.8) gate on query intent detection before boosting. Layer 4.525 has NO intent gating — it fires for every query unconditionally.
fractal_analysis.sibling_comparison: Layer 4.5 (Attribute Boost) requires attribute_boost_ids from Layer 0.5 analysis. Layer 4.55 (Temporal Boost) requires temporal_gate. Layer 4.525 requires only non-empty query_stems — effectively ungated.
fractal_analysis.mirror_test: BREAKS pattern. Every other boost layer has a semantic gate. This one fires on syntactic overlap alone.
fractal_analysis.scale_consistency: At corpus scale (60 memories), false positives are manageable. At production scale (10K+ memories), single-stem overlap becomes pure noise.

hazop.MORE_overlap: Lower guard (>=1) means more false positive boosts. Single common stem like 'chang' matches memories about database changes, architecture changes, process changes — all get +0.10 when query is about one specific change.
hazop.LESS_filtering: Stop word list is too aggressive for IR queries. Stripping 'use', 'did', 'what' removes query intent signals. LESS filtering would retain discriminative query terms.
hazop.OTHER_THAN_stemming: Porter stemming is lossy. BM25 already handles stemming internally. Applying stemming a second time in a separate layer creates double-stemming artifacts.
hazop.REVERSE_boost_direction: Some memories should be PENALIZED for entity overlap when the overlap is generic (e.g., 'change' appears in 15 memories). Current boost is always positive.

fmea.failure: Indiscriminate entity overlap boost pollutes top-5 rankings for single_hop queries
fmea.notes: Already manifesting: single_hop MRR dropped to 0.500. 3 of 5 single_hop queries retrieve wrong memories in top position because generic-stem memories got boosted above the precise answer.

edge_analysis.data_crossing: Query text → keyword scan → temporal_gate bool; temporal_gate → 4 fallback strategies → temporal_fact_boost_ids: HashSet<MemoryId>
edge_analysis.control_flow: Gate fires on has_any_temporal_intent OR has_temporal_keywords. Keywords include 'meeting', 'session', 'phase', 'happened' which appear in non-temporal queries.
failure_modes: CRITICAL: 'What happened during the debugging phase?' has 'happened' AND 'phase' → temporal gate fires. But 'What bugs did we find during the debugging phase?' is classified as temporal in the benchmark. The gate correctly fires but Strategy 2 (entity-only) floods with unrelated temporal facts.

Strategy 2 (entity-only, limit 50) returns ALL facts for an entity regardless of relevance. For entities like 'Raj' who appears in many sessions, this injects many irrelevant source memories into boost set.
Strategy 4 (time-window scan, limit 200) is effectively unbounded for small corpora. Scans ALL facts and boosts ALL that fall in window.
'meeting' keyword matches queries about 'What happened during the second meeting?' — correct temporal intent. But also matches 'What was discussed about meetings in general?' if such queries existed.
edge_analysis.contracts: Returns HashSet of MemoryIds to boost. No upper bound on set size. Strategy 2 can return up to 50 facts × N source memories each.

fractal_analysis.pattern_at_scale: Strategies 1→4 form a widening fallback cascade. Each strategy is less precise than the previous. Strategy 4 (scan all facts) is basically 'boost everything temporal'.
fractal_analysis.mirror_test: BREAKS pattern — other layers narrow candidates as they progress. This layer WIDENS candidates through fallback cascade.
fractal_analysis.scale_consistency: At 60 memories, the fallback cascade is tolerable. At production scale, Strategy 2 (50 facts) and Strategy 4 (200 facts) would inject massive noise.

hazop.MORE_strategies: 4 fallback strategies where 1-2 would suffice. Each additional strategy widens the temporal boost set, diluting precision.
hazop.EARLY_firing: Temporal gate fires on keyword match before query analysis confirms temporal intent. 'session' in 'what happened during the debugging session' triggers temporal path even if query is about content, not time.
hazop.PART_OF_facts: Strategy 2 returns ALL entity facts without event filtering — returns facts about entity's preferences, relationships, demographics in addition to the relevant temporal event.

fmea.failure: Temporal gate over-fires, boosting unrelated temporal-source memories into top-5 for temporal AND non-temporal queries
fmea.notes: 3 absence violations in temporal category. Temporal boost injects session-1 memories into session-2/3 queries because entity-only lookup has no session filtering.

edge_analysis.data_crossing: Query text → decision keyword detection → ExperienceType lookup per fused memory → +0.15 boost for Decision type
edge_analysis.control_flow: Fires when query contains 'choose', 'chose', 'decide', 'pick', 'select', 'what did we', or ('what' AND 'use').
failure_modes: ('what' AND 'use') is extremely broad. 'What database are we using?' triggers decision boost even though it's asking about current state, not the decision itself. This boosts Memory[3] (PostgreSQL decision, ExperienceType::Decision) AND Memory[18] (MongoDB switch, ExperienceType::Decision) — correct! But also boosts Memory[58] (SDK decision) and Memory[52] (new decision process) which are unrelated decisions.
'What testing framework did we pick?' triggers on 'pick'. Correctly boosts Memory[4]. But also boosts all other Decision-type memories in the corpus.

'what did we' matches 'What trade-offs did we discuss about the backend language choice?' — triggers decision boost for a multi_hop query, potentially helping. But also matches 'What decisions did we make last week?' (temporal query), pulling in non-recent decisions.

fractal_analysis.pattern_at_scale: Decision boost is query-type-discriminated (good), but the discrimination is too broad. 'what' + 'use' catches many non-decision queries.
fractal_analysis.mirror_test: Pattern matches sibling layers — intent detection → type-specific boost. But the detection granularity is coarser than Layer 4.5 (Attribute Boost) which uses parsed query structure, not keyword matching.

hazop.MORE_keywords: Adding 'what did we' expanded coverage but reduced precision. Many queries start with 'what did we' without asking about decisions.
hazop.OTHER_THAN_decision: No equivalent boost exists for other experience types (Task, Learning, Observation, Discovery). Asymmetric boosting favors Decision-type memories in ranking.

edge_analysis.data_crossing: Query text + QueryAnalysis → sub-queries (Vec<String>) → per-sub-query vector search → weighted RRF merge
edge_analysis.control_flow: Decomposition fires on conjunction ('AND'), multi-entity, or comparison queries. Sub-queries are separately embedded and searched with max_results * 8 pool.
failure_modes: RRF merge uses max-merge but the formula at line 2548-2551 is confused: `*entry = entry.max(rrf_score).max(*entry); *entry = *entry + rrf_score - entry.min(rrf_score);` — the max() already computed is overwritten by the additive correction. This effectively adds rrf_score minus the minimum of (current, rrf_score), which is just rrf_score when entry > rrf_score. The intent was max-merge but the implementation is additive.

Sub-query weight (0.6) means sub-queries contribute significantly. For 'Why did we change the database AND what was the impact on the timeline?', the sub-query 'what was the impact on the timeline?' pulls in timeline-related memories that may not be about database impact specifically.

fractal_analysis.pattern_at_scale: Decomposition follows the ColBERT/DSP pattern of query-level decomposition → independent retrieval → fusion. Well-established IR pattern.
fractal_analysis.mirror_test: HOLDS — consistent with how BM25 is already doing compound query handling. Decomposition adds value for vector search which embeds the full query as one vector.
fractal_analysis.scale_consistency: Each sub-query triggers a full vector search (max_results * 8). At 4 sub-queries, this is 4x the vector search cost. Latency impact visible but acceptable (<50ms total).

edge_analysis.data_crossing: Fused scores → content length analysis → multiplicative penalty (0.70-1.15x)
edge_analysis.control_flow: Fires when 5+ candidates exist. Penalizes content >1.5x mean length.
failure_modes: Memory[59] (lessons learned summary, 353 chars) and Memory[55] (tech debt inventory, 342 chars) are medium-length but contain exactly the right answers for open_domain queries. They're close to 1.5x mean if mean is ~230 chars.

The penalty is multiplicative on fused score, not additive. A 0.70x penalty on a boosted score still preserves relative ranking within category, but can pull a correctly-ranked memory below an incorrectly-boosted shorter memory.

fractal_analysis.pattern_at_scale: Length-based heuristic is a reasonable proxy for specificity in structured corpora but fails for corpora with naturally variable length.
fractal_analysis.mirror_test: HOLDS — other layers use content features (entities, type, temporal) but this is the only one using content length. Adds orthogonal signal.

phase_2_fractal_atam.core_pattern: Additive boost layers stacked on RRF fusion. Each layer adds to fused score based on different signals (entity overlap, temporal facts, experience type, specificity, interference, ontology). Final ranking determined by sum of all boosts.
phase_2_fractal_atam.macro_consistency: Pipeline follows a clear pattern: pre-compute boost signals (Layers 0.5-0.7) → vector search (Layer 3) → BM25 fusion (Layer 4) → apply boosts (Layers 4.5-4.9) → rerank (Layer 5). This is architecturally sound.

meso_breaks.details: Every other boost layer has semantic gating (attribute detection, temporal intent, decision keywords, entity presence). Layer 4.525 fires unconditionally on non-empty query stems. This is the only ungated boost in the pipeline.
meso_breaks.impact: Pollutes rankings for all query types. Most visible on single_hop where the correct answer has fewer entity tags than retrospective/summary memories.

meso_breaks.break: Temporal gate expansion contradicts narrowing pattern
meso_breaks.details: Layers 0.5, 0.6, 0.7 are supposed to narrow candidates for precise boosting. The 4-strategy fallback cascade in Layer 0.6 WIDENS candidates instead. Strategy 2 (entity-only, limit 50) and Strategy 4 (scan all, limit 200) defeat the narrowing purpose.
meso_breaks.impact: Temporal boost set becomes noisy, injecting non-relevant temporal-source memories. Visible as 3 absence violations in temporal category.

meso_breaks.break: Additive boost magnitudes are not calibrated relative to base RRF scores
meso_breaks.details: Base RRF scores range ~0.01-0.05 (1/(20+rank)). Boost magnitudes: attribute=0.50, temporal=0.20, decision=0.15, entity_overlap=0.10-0.40, ontology=0.08-0.25. A single entity overlap match (+0.10) is 2-10x the base RRF score, dominating the semantic ranking signal.
meso_breaks.impact: Boosts overwhelm base ranking. Memories with high semantic similarity but no entity overlap lose to memories with low semantic similarity but one entity match.

tradeoffs.description: Lowering entity overlap guard from >=2 to >=1 improved recall for rare-entity queries (fixing 4/7 failures) but degraded precision for common-entity queries. Net: +4 fixes, -3 regressions.
tradeoffs.sensitivity: Highly sensitive to guard threshold. At >=2, misses single-entity queries. At >=1, over-fires on common stems.

tradeoffs.description: 4-strategy temporal fallback cascade ensures no temporal query goes unanswered but injects noise. Strategy 1 (entity+event) is precise. Strategy 4 (time-window scan) is essentially random within time window.
tradeoffs.sensitivity: Removing strategies 3-4 would improve precision but miss queries where entity parsing fails.

challenges.scenario: All 5 multi_hop queries happen to be conjunction queries ('AND', 'and why', 'based on') that decomposition handles perfectly. A multi_hop query without explicit conjunction (e.g., 'How did Raj's architecture proposal affect our timeline?') would likely score lower because decomposition wouldn't fire.
challenges.evidence: decompose_query only splits on 'AND', 'and also', 'as well as', multi-entity, or comparison patterns. Implicit multi-hop queries (requiring inference across memories without explicit conjunction) would not decompose.
challenges.adjustment: Score holds for current benchmark. Real-world multi_hop MRR would be lower (~0.7-0.8) with implicit multi-hop queries.
challenges.revised_score: 1.000 (benchmark valid, but ceiling effect — test set is favorable to decomposition)

challenges.scenario: Open domain queries are broad ('Summarize the project', 'What are the biggest risks?'). These benefit from the entity overlap boost because broad queries have many stem matches, and retrospective/summary memories have many entities. The boost is helping here, not hurting.
challenges.evidence: Memory[59] (lessons learned, 8 entity tags) and Memory[11] (risk assessment) have high entity overlap with broad queries. The same boost that hurts single_hop helps open_domain.
challenges.adjustment: No adjustment. The boost's broad matching is genuinely useful for broad queries.

correctness.evidence_code: Layer 4.525 stop word list strips query-semantic terms ('use', 'did', 'what'). RRF merge formula (L2548-2551) has confused max/additive logic. Temporal gate keyword list includes non-temporal terms ('meeting', 'session').
correctness.evidence_structural: Entity overlap boost is ungated — violates the gating pattern established by all other boost layers. Fallback cascade widens instead of narrows.

robustness.evidence_code: BLOCKER: Single-stem overlap gives +0.10 to any memory with one common term. At production corpus size (10K+), nearly every memory would get boosted. Strategy 4 scans 200 facts without relevance filtering.
robustness.evidence_structural: No upper bound on temporal_fact_boost_ids set size. No normalization of boost magnitude against base scores. Additive boosts compound — worst case: 0.50 (attr) + 0.40 (entity) + 0.20 (temporal) + 0.15 (decision) + 0.25 (ontology) = 1.50 boost on a 0.05 base score.

performance.evidence_code: Latency 32-60ms per query. Vector search 4x pool, BM25 fusion, 30-candidate rerank pool. Stemmer instantiated per-query (could be cached).
performance.evidence_structural: Query decomposition adds N-1 embedding computations per compound query. Each sub-query searches max_results*8 candidates. Linear in corpus size via vector index, manageable.

security.evidence_code: No user input reaches shell. Entity matching is pure string comparison. Temporal fact lookup uses typed API (find_by_entity_filtered, find_by_event).
security.evidence_structural: Entity-stuffed memories could game overlap boost — low severity in single-user system.

data_integrity.evidence_code: All boost operations are additive/multiplicative on in-memory HashMap. No persistent state mutation during recall. Temporal fact lookup is read-only.
data_integrity.evidence_structural: Fused scores are ephemeral (per-query). No risk of corrupting stored data through recall path.

maintainability.evidence_code: semantic_retrieve() is ~1400 lines with 15 numbered layers. Layer numbering gaps (4.5, 4.52, 4.525, 4.53, 4.55) make insertion ordering non-obvious. Boost constants scattered across constants.rs and inline const declarations.
maintainability.evidence_structural: Adding a new boost layer requires understanding all existing layers' magnitudes to avoid score pollution. No framework for A/B testing different boost combinations.

scalability.evidence_code: Entity overlap iterates all fused candidates × all entity stems. Temporal fallback Strategy 2 fetches up to 50 facts. Strategy 4 scans up to 200. These are O(candidates × entities) and O(facts) respectively.
scalability.evidence_structural: At 10K memories, fused candidates could be 500+. Entity overlap becomes O(500 × avg_entities). Temporal scan becomes O(all_facts). Neither is indexed.

testability.evidence_code: LOCOMO benchmark covers 20 queries across 4 types with MRR/R@5/P@5/absence metrics. Per-query detail output enables regression analysis. Tests are deterministic (no randomness in scoring).
testability.evidence_structural: No per-layer isolation testing. Cannot test Layer 4.525 independently — must run full pipeline. No ablation framework to measure individual layer contribution.

compliance.evidence_code: Recall path is read-only. No PII handling in scoring layers. Temporal facts lookup respects user_id scoping.
compliance.evidence_structural: Memory isolation by user_id enforced at storage layer, propagated through all lookup calls.

observability.evidence_code: Every layer has tracing::info! or tracing::debug! with layer number, count of affected memories, and timing. Cumulative latency tracked at each stage.
observability.evidence_structural: Missing: per-layer score delta logging. Cannot see HOW MUCH each layer changed rankings without adding debug output. Would enable rapid regression diagnosis.

extensibility.evidence_code: New boost layers require: adding to constants.rs, implementing in semantic_retrieve(), calibrating magnitude against all other layers, updating LOCOMO benchmark expectations.
extensibility.evidence_structural: No plugin/trait-based boost system. Each layer is inline code in a single function. Boost layers can't be conditionally enabled/disabled without code changes.

usability.evidence_code: Benchmark output is clear and actionable. Per-query detail shows expected vs retrieved. Composite score provides single number for comparison. Reference to mem0 (66.9%) gives external context.
usability.evidence_structural: No configuration surface for tuning boost weights without recompilation. Constants are compile-time.

blockers.reason: Single-stem entity overlap boost with no gating pollutes rankings at scale. Temporal fallback cascade has unbounded noise injection. Boost magnitudes are uncalibrated against base RRF scores.

quality.notes: Entity overlap and temporal gate expansions introduced regressions. Code quality in merge logic (L2548-2551) is confused.

completeness.notes: Pipeline covers all retrieval dimensions: semantic, lexical, graph, temporal, entity, type, ontological. No major signal category missing.

performance.notes: Sub-50ms latency across all query types. Decomposition adds acceptable overhead.

utility.notes: 64.3% composite is competitive but below mem0 (66.9%). Single_hop regression hurts core use case.

coherence.notes: Layer 4.525 breaks the gating pattern. Temporal cascade breaks the narrowing pattern. Decision boost detection is inconsistent granularity.

cohesiveness.notes: 15 layers with ad-hoc numbering. Boost magnitudes set independently without cross-layer calibration.

elasticity.notes: No runtime tuning. Constants are compile-time. No A/B framework. Adding/removing layers requires full pipeline understanding.

function.notes: All layers execute correctly per their individual logic. The issue is interaction effects, not individual layer bugs.

ease_of_use.notes: Benchmark and tracing output are clear. Lack of per-layer ablation makes diagnosis harder than necessary.

failing_queries.query: What programming language did we choose for the backend?
failing_queries.root_cause: Layer 4.52 fires ('what' + 'use' not present, but 'choose' triggers decision boost). Memory[2] IS Decision type, so boost helps. But Layer 4.525 stems 'program'→'program', 'languag'→'languag', 'choos'→'choos'. Multiple retrospective memories (50, 51, 59) contain stemmed matches for 'languag' or 'choos' and get +0.10-0.20 entity overlap boost, competing with Memory[2].
failing_queries.likely_fix: Restore >=2 overlap guard for Layer 4.525. Memory[2] has entities ['rust', 'backend', 'language', 'decision'] which give 2+ overlaps. Retrospective memories have 1 incidental overlap.

failing_queries.root_cause: Layer 4.52 fires on ('what' AND 'use') → decision boost applies to ALL Decision-type memories. Memory[3] and [18] are both Decision type (correct). But Memory[46] (analytics back to PostgreSQL), Memory[49] (CRDT decision), Memory[52] (RFC process), Memory[58] (SDK decision) are also Decision type and get +0.15 each. Layer 4.525: 'databas'→'databas' stem matches Memory[3] entities. But 'use' is in stop word list — stripped. Query becomes {'databas'} with 1 stem. Guard >=1 means all memories with 'database' anywhere in entities get +0.10.
failing_queries.likely_fix: Remove 'use' from stop words. Add frequency-based filtering: if a stem appears in >30% of memory entities, treat it as corpus-level stop word, not query-level.

failing_queries.query: What was the first bug we encountered?
failing_queries.root_cause: Stems: 'first'→'first', 'bug'→'bug', 'encount'→'encount'. 'what' and 'was' stripped. Memory[30] entities include 'bug' — gets +0.10. But Memory[48] (microservice bugs), Memory[39] (CI bugs), Memory[55] (tech debt mentioning bugs) also match 'bug' stem. All get +0.10. Memory[30] needs 'first' to differentiate — 'first' is NOT in stop words, but Memory[30] entities likely don't include 'first' as an entity. Result: Memory[30] ties with other bug memories on entity overlap, relies on vector similarity to differentiate.
failing_queries.likely_fix: Entity overlap should not boost when the overlap is on a high-frequency corpus term ('bug' appears in 5+ memories). IDF-weighting would downweight common entity stems.

failing_queries.query: What decisions did we make last week?
failing_queries.root_cause: Temporal gate fires (has 'last week'). Strategy 1: entity='decisions' (from query analysis), event_keywords from verbs. But 'decisions' is a concept, not a named entity — temporal fact lookup likely returns nothing. Strategy 2: entity-only lookup for 'decisions' — returns facts about decision-making across ALL sessions, not just session 4. All decision-source memories get temporal boost (+0.20), including session-1 decisions.
failing_queries.likely_fix: Strategy 2 needs session/time filtering. If query says 'last week', entity-only facts should be filtered to the most recent session.

failing_queries.query: What happened during the second meeting?
failing_queries.root_cause: Temporal gate fires on 'during' and 'meeting'. Strategy 1: entity='second meeting' or 'meeting'. Event keywords may include 'happened'. Temporal fact store may not have facts indexed under 'second meeting'. Fallback to Strategy 2/3/4 which don't have session-ordinal reasoning ('second' = session 2).
failing_queries.likely_fix: Query parser should map ordinal session references ('second meeting', 'first session') to session indices. Temporal facts need session_id association.

failing_queries.query: What bugs did we find during the debugging phase?
failing_queries.root_cause: Temporal gate fires on 'during' and 'phase'. 'debugging phase' maps to session 3. But temporal fact lookup may return bug-related facts from ALL sessions. Layer 4.525: 'bug' stem matches multiple memories. 3 absence violations in temporal suggest wrong-session memories appearing in results.
failing_queries.likely_fix: Need session-phase mapping: 'debugging phase' → session 3. Filter temporal facts by session.

absence_violations.cause: Temporal boost applies to ALL temporal-source memories matching entity, regardless of session. Queries about specific sessions/phases retrieve memories from wrong sessions.

priority_1_entity_overlap_guard.change: Restore >=2 overlap guard. Remove 'use', 'did' from stop words (they're query-semantic). Add IDF-weighting: stems appearing in >25% of candidate entities get downweighted.
priority_1_entity_overlap_guard.expected_impact: single_hop MRR 0.500 → 0.700+. Reduces false-positive boosts from single common-stem matches.
priority_1_entity_overlap_guard.risk: May re-regress the 4/7 fixes from 3b0f067. Mitigation: IDF-weighting preserves rare-entity matching while suppressing common-entity noise.

priority_2_temporal_session_filtering.change: Strategy 2 and 4: filter returned facts by session/time-window when query has session reference. Reduce Strategy 2 limit from 50 to 20. Remove Strategy 4 (time-window scan) — too noisy.
priority_2_temporal_session_filtering.expected_impact: temporal MRR 0.533 → 0.700+. Eliminates cross-session temporal boost contamination. Fixes 3 absence violations.
priority_2_temporal_session_filtering.risk: Removing Strategy 4 may miss edge-case temporal queries where entity parsing fails. Acceptable given Strategy 3 (event-keyword-only) as fallback.

priority_3_decision_boost_narrowing.change: Remove ('what' AND 'use') trigger — too broad. Keep explicit decision verbs ('choose', 'chose', 'decide', 'pick', 'select').
priority_3_decision_boost_narrowing.expected_impact: Reduces false-positive decision boost on 'What database are we using?' type queries. Minor improvement to single_hop precision.
priority_3_decision_boost_narrowing.risk: May miss decision queries phrased as 'What did we use for X?'. Low risk — BM25 and vector search handle these well without type boost.

priority_4_boost_magnitude_calibration.change: Normalize all boost magnitudes relative to typical base RRF score. At K=20, rank-1 RRF is 0.048, rank-5 is 0.040. A +0.10 entity overlap boost is equivalent to jumping 2 RRF ranks. A +0.50 attribute boost is equivalent to jumping 10+ ranks. Rebalance so strongest boost (attribute) is ~5 rank positions and weakest (entity overlap) is ~1 rank position.
priority_4_boost_magnitude_calibration.expected_impact: Prevents any single boost layer from dominating rankings. Improves score distribution.
priority_4_boost_magnitude_calibration.risk: Requires full LOCOMO re-evaluation after calibration. May affect multi_hop and open_domain scores.

summary.diagnosis: The single_hop and temporal regressions are caused by two interacting issues: (1) Layer 4.525 entity overlap boost fires too broadly after the guard was lowered to >=1 stem match, and the stop word list strips semantically important query terms. (2) The temporal gate expansion with 4 fallback strategies over-injects temporal-source memories without session filtering, causing cross-session contamination visible as 3 absence violations.
summary.multi_hop_status: MRR 1.000 is genuine — query decomposition works correctly for the current benchmark. However, the benchmark is favorable to decomposition (all queries have explicit conjunctions). Implicit multi-hop queries would likely score lower.
summary.open_domain_status: MRR 0.900 benefits from the same entity overlap boost that hurts single_hop. Broad queries match many stems, and summary/retrospective memories have many entities — the boost correctly surfaces these for broad queries.
summary.composite_gap: 64.3% vs mem0's 66.9% (-2.6pp). Fixing single_hop (+0.200 MRR) and temporal (+0.167 MRR) would push composite to ~72%, clearing mem0.
summary.architectural_concern: The additive boost architecture has reached a complexity cliff. Each new layer interacts with all previous layers in non-obvious ways. The next improvement should consider either (a) a unified scoring model that learns weights, or (b) per-layer ablation testing to quantify individual layer contribution before adding new layers.
